text: Most networking science research has dealt with small-scale systems. Recently, mobile phone data has started to appear as new sources of interesting graph structures. The study of such data has become a major research area over the last decade. Analyzing mobile phone data started as an analysis of mobile phone call records. Large sets of anonymous call records become available and the social, spatial and temporal structure hidden in the data could be exposed and analyzed in details. This led to a major breakthrough in sociology since mobile phones can be seen as a sensor monitoring activities of individuals. For the first time in history sociologists are able to have measurements at the society wide level, rather than only basing their results from surveys on small groups of individuals. This survey attempts to summarize the most important results that have been achieved in the analysis of mobile phone data. First of all, we look at the social network structures that can be derived from call and messaging data; then we look at the behavior and patterns of individuals and groups when it comes to tracking mobility; finally we look at the aspects related to spatial information such as clustering and urban planning applications and their uses in aid of international development and public health while also discussing data protection issues.
explanation: The text is coherent and reads like a scholarly survey with plausible domain claims; small stylistic quirks and lack of technical specifics keep the probability uncertain, slightly favoring human authorship.
